title: Early steps in the civil rights movement, 1940s and 1950s
description: Test your skills on the rise of the civil rights movement in the 1940s and 1950s!
topic_path: Early steps in the civil rights movement, 1940s and 1950s | 8.6
Early steps in the civil rights movement, 1940s and 1950s

assessment_items.question_content:
> “President Eisenhower was well aware of the political costs of intervening in the Little Rock situation — or not intervening. He knew for one thing that the leadership in the Congress, both the Senate and the House, would be antagonized if he decided to send troops into Little Rock. . . But he knew that not going into Little Rock would mean that he would be charged, and rightfully charged, with not enforcing the Supreme Court decision in the *Brown* case. Upholding the Constitution was his duty as president, so on balance he never had any hesitation once the crisis developed.” 

> -Source: Herbert Brownwell, historian, *Voices of Freedom: An Oral History of the Civil Rights Movement from the 1950s Through the 1980s,* 2011

**The author’s remarks in the excerpt most directly reflected which of the following developments during the mid-twentieth century?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the legal and political successes of desegregation
the growth in power and influence for the presidency
the grassroots efforts aimed at eliminating discrimination
the emergence of the military-industrial complex

assessment_items.question_content:
> “President Eisenhower was well aware of the political costs of intervening in the Little Rock situation — or not intervening. He knew for one thing that the leadership in the Congress, both the Senate and the House, would be antagonized if he decided to send troops into Little Rock. . . But he knew that not going into Little Rock would mean that he would be charged, and rightfully charged, with not enforcing the Supreme Court decision in the *Brown* case. Upholding the Constitution was his duty as president, so on balance he never had any hesitation once the crisis developed.” 

> -Source: Herbert Brownwell, historian, *Voices of Freedom: An Oral History of the Civil Rights Movement from the 1950s Through the 1980s,* 2011

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the desegregation of public schools
the integration of the American military
the emergence of a bus boycott
the rise of protests against the Korean War

assessment_items.question_content:
> “President Eisenhower was well aware of the political costs of intervening in the Little Rock situation — or not intervening. He knew for one thing that the leadership in the Congress, both the Senate and the House, would be antagonized if he decided to send troops into Little Rock. . . But he knew that not going into Little Rock would mean that he would be charged, and rightfully charged, with not enforcing the Supreme Court decision in the *Brown* case. Upholding the Constitution was his duty as president, so on balance he never had any hesitation once the crisis developed.” 

> -Source: Herbert Brownwell, historian, *Voices of Freedom: An Oral History of the Civil Rights Movement from the 1950s Through the 1980s,* 2011

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the work of the NAACP to challenge segregation through the judicial branch
the protests and boycotts led by Martin Luther King, Jr.
the fundraising power and political influence of African Americans
the ratification of the Equal Rights Amendment to end Jim Crow segregation

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The protest is keeping the people off the bus, and we know it is more than successful. A number of people are working from more than full time to part time to operate a transportation system inspite [sic] of all obstacles placed against us. The people have walked when they could not get rides in the most inclimate [sic] weather. Many are still saying they will walk forever before they will go back to riding the bus under the same conditions.” 

>-Source: Rosa Parks, Rosa Parks Papers, [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/mss85943.001505/?sp=2&r=0.052,0.411,0.693,0.571,0), 1955-1956

**The protest described in the passage is best known as the:**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Montgomery Bus Boycott
Freedom Rides
March to Montgomery
Greensboro sit-in

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The protest is keeping the people off the bus, and we know it is more than successful. A number of people are working from more than full time to part time to operate a transportation system inspite [sic] of all obstacles placed against us. The people have walked when they could not get rides in the most inclimate [sic] weather. Many are still saying they will walk forever before they will go back to riding the bus under the same conditions.” 

>-Source: Rosa Parks, Rosa Parks Papers, [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/mss85943.001505/?sp=2&r=0.052,0.411,0.693,0.571,0), 1955-1956

**The tactics described in the excerpt best represent which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: using nonviolent boycotts to fight against unjust treatment
running for local office to eliminate racial inequalities
sending legal challenges to segregation to the Supreme Court
harnessing televised reports to gain public sympathy

assessment_items.question_content:
> “The protest is keeping the people off the bus, and we know it is more than successful. A number of people are working from more than full time to part time to operate a transportation system inspite [sic] of all obstacles placed against us. The people have walked when they could not get rides in the most inclimate [sic] weather. Many are still saying they will walk forever before they will go back to riding the bus under the same conditions.” 

>-Source: Rosa Parks, Rosa Parks Papers, [Library of Congress](https://www.loc.gov/resource/mss85943.001505/?sp=2&r=0.052,0.411,0.693,0.571,0), 1955-1956

**The events described in the excerpt contributed most directly to which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964
the Supreme Court decision in *Plessy v. Ferguson*
the ratification of the Twenty-fourth Amendment
the establishment of the NAACP

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Truman’s commitment to civil rights, and perhaps most significantly his desegregation of the armed forces, brought him important political benefits. For one thing, most African Americans who voted supported Truman in 1948. Perhaps most significantly, Truman’s desegregation of the armed forces helped offset negative perceptions of the Democratic Party caused by Southern segregationists in Congress and elsewhere, and it helped prevent the Republican Party from regaining its pre-New Deal level of support among African Americans.”

>-Source: Tom Lansford and Richard M. Yon, historians, “Political Pragmatism and Civil Rights Policy,” *The Civil Rights Legacy of Harry S. Truman,*  2007

**Which of the following evidence would best support the authors’ argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: data showing the demographic makeup of voters for each candidate in presidential elections
data showing the average income of active duty service members before and after 1948
data showing the voting rate for African American voters in presidential and midterm elections
data showing the popular vote results in presidential elections following the election of 1948

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Truman’s commitment to civil rights, and perhaps most significantly his desegregation of the armed forces, brought him important political benefits. For one thing, most African Americans who voted supported Truman in 1948. Perhaps most significantly, Truman’s desegregation of the armed forces helped offset negative perceptions of the Democratic Party caused by Southern segregationists in Congress and elsewhere, and it helped prevent the Republican Party from regaining its pre-New Deal level of support among African Americans.”

>-Source: Tom Lansford and Richard M. Yon, historians, “Political Pragmatism and Civil Rights Policy,” *The Civil Rights Legacy of Harry S. Truman,*  2007

**According to the passage, which of the following best explains the most important effect that desegregation of the military had on the United States?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It ensured that the black community would support the Democratic Party.
It led to the end of the Republican Party’s supremacy in federal politics.
It improved the socio-economic positions for most African Americans.
It caused a realignment of the Democratic and Republican parties.

assessment_items.question_content:
> “Truman’s commitment to civil rights, and perhaps most significantly his desegregation of the armed forces, brought him important political benefits. For one thing, most African Americans who voted supported Truman in 1948. Perhaps most significantly, Truman’s desegregation of the armed forces helped offset negative perceptions of the Democratic Party caused by Southern segregationists in Congress and elsewhere, and it helped prevent the Republican Party from regaining its pre-New Deal level of support among African Americans.”

>-Source: Tom Lansford and Richard M. Yon, historians, “Political Pragmatism and Civil Rights Policy,” *The Civil Rights Legacy of Harry S. Truman,*  2007

**Which of the following most directly led to the changes described in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the issuance of Executive Order 9981
the decision in *Brown v. Board of Education*
the ratification of the Twenty-fourth Amendment
the passage of the GI Bill

assessment_items.question_content:
> “So now the court has found in our favor and recognized our human psychological complexity and citizenship and another battle of the Civil War has been won. The rest is up to us and I am very glad. . . So now the judges found Negroes must be individuals and that is hopeful and good. What a wonderful world of possibilities are unfolded for the children.” 

>-Source: Ralph Waldo Ellison, in a letter, 1954

**People who shared Ellison’s ideas at the time most typically sought to achieve their goals by doing which of the following? **

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: using nonviolent protests and boycotts
voting against segregationist candidates
proposing local and state ballot initiatives
promoting the Equal Rights Amendment

assessment_items.question_content:
> “So now the court has found in our favor and recognized our human psychological complexity and citizenship and another battle of the Civil War has been won. The rest is up to us and I am very glad. . . So now the judges found Negroes must be individuals and that is hopeful and good. What a wonderful world of possibilities are unfolded for the children.” 

>-Source: Ralph Waldo Ellison, in a letter, 1954

**The excerpt is best understood as a response to which of the following historical developments?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the decision in *Brown v. Board of Education*
the desegregation of the armed forces
the ratification of the Fourteenth Amendment
the success of the bus boycott in Montgomery

assessment_items.question_content:
> “So now the court has found in our favor and recognized our human psychological complexity and citizenship and another battle of the Civil War has been won. The rest is up to us and I am very glad. . . So now the judges found Negroes must be individuals and that is hopeful and good. What a wonderful world of possibilities are unfolded for the children.” 

>-Source: Ralph Waldo Ellison, in a letter, 1954

**In the decades following *Brown v. Board of Education* (1954), the African American Civil Rights Movement focused its energies most strongly on which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: ending racial discrimination in public places
achieving the right to vote for African Americans
gaining representation in Congress
addressing the causes of mass poverty
